item.title: SteamOS 3.8 just dramatically improved a lot of handhelds except the Steam Deck itself
item.itunes:title: SteamOS 3.8 just dramatically improved a lot of handhelds except the Steam Deck itself
item.itunes:author: XDA[RSS+]
item.itunes:summary:
The Steam Deck just saw one of its biggest updates enter the preview phase, and if you're on the Preview update channel, then chances are you've already updated it. After such a major update, you might realize that not much has changed, though. SteamOS 3.8 is, by all accounts, one of Valve's most important updates yet. It expands compatibility, improves performance, and lays the groundwork for something much bigger than a handheld.

And yet, on the Deck itself, this massive new update doesn't feel like a huge leap forward. That's not a bad thing, either. Instead, what it reveals is a shift now, because from the looks of it, SteamOS isn't really about the Steam Deck anymore. It's about everything else it's about to run on.

I used Bazzite instead of SteamOS on my gaming PC, and it does everything SteamOS can't

SteamOS is excellent for gaming, but as soon as you try and do anything else Bazzite is a clear winner

11
By  Ty Sherback

SteamOS is finally breaking out of the Deck

This update signals a platform, not a product

For years now, SteamOS and the Steam Deck have been downright inseparable. One justified the other, after all. With version 3.8, however, that relationship starts to loosen in a rather intentional way. This is the first update that feels like it was designed with hardware beyond Valve's own handheld in mind. Under the hood, SteamOS 3.8 comes bearing support for a wider range of AMD and Intel platforms, along with better handling of discrete GPUs and varied input configurations.

Now, none of these are upgrades that the Steam Deck needs, but they do lay the groundwork for devices that don't even exist yet, and for those that don't ship with SteamOS preinstalled. You can feel Valve widening the lens here. SteamOS is no longer being tuned exclusively for one fixed hardware target. It's being shaped into a flexible, scalable platform now. In doing so, we can see the Steam Deck repositioning itself in the gaming handheld universe, becoming the center of it instead of being just a very well-supported member of it.

Other handhelds gain what they were missing

SteamOS 3.8 feels transformative, but not for the Deck per se

Spend a few days with a Windows-powered handheld, and the cracks will begin to show. Devices like the ROG Ally or Legion Go are undeniably powerful, but the experience often feels like you're forcing a desktop OS into something it was never designed for. Tiny UI elements, inconsistent controller navigation, sleep that doesn't always behave, and background processes quietly eating into battery life. It sure works, but it rarely feels finished.

SteamOS 3.8 directly smooths over many of those rough edges. Improved hardware compatibility and driver support mean fewer weird quirks and better game consistency across different chipsets. More importantly, the interface itself changes how these devices feel to use. Instant suspend/resume behaves predictably, controller-first navigation is seamless across the entire system, and you won't be fighting the OS anymore, just to launch or switch games.

There's also a noticeable improvement in consistency with 3.8. Without Windows constantly juggling updates, services, and background tasks, these handhelds tend to deliver more stable frame pacing and slightly better battery behavior in real-world use. This is just a leaner, purpose-built OS doing less unnecessary work. For the Steam Deck, this serves as refinement, but for other handhelds, SteamOS 3.8 is what finally makes the hardware feel like it belongs.

SteamOS proved the Linux desktop works when you stop trying to make it look like Windows

SteamOS hasn't brought about "the great exodus" to Linux, but it does teach distros how to do it right

9
By  Samarveer Singh

These "big" features aren't all that big on the Deck

They're quality-of-life wins, not game changers

On paper, SteamOS 3.8 brings a handful of long-requested features like proper hibernation, improved Bluetooth audio support (including microphone support), and Wayland's enhancements to external display handling like HDR and VRR. Of course these are all welcome additions, but they're also not transformative; at least not on the Deck. That's because the Steam Deck already nailed the fundamentals.

Its suspend-and-resume behavior has been rock solid for years, making hibernation feel more of a checkbox feature rather than an outright necessity. Even the Bluetooth improvements are nice, but they address edge cases rather than everyday frustrations. Even HDR and VRR support, while technically impressive, are most relevant only in docked scenarios that many Deck users only occasionally engage with. That doesn't make these upgrades insignificant, either. Instead, it puts them in perspective. Steam OS 3.8 clearly signifies that the Steam Deck doesn't need to be reinvented, and it's now time for the experience to expand outward.

Someone turned their Steam Deck into an awesome DS-like gaming machine

And it didn't require any modifications to the internals.

1
By  Simon Batt

This is more of a living room update

It sets the stage for the Steam Machine

It might be really beneficial for other non-Valve handhelds, and barely anything major for the Steam Deck itself, but SteamOS 3.8 is certainly here to set the stage for the upcoming Steam machine. The improvements to desktop mode, display scaling, external monitor handling, and broader GPU compatibility all point in the same direction: Valve wants more than just the handheld game. It's preparing SteamOS for bigger screens, different use cases, and a more traditional console-like setup.

HDR and VRR support that comes with version 3.8 is essential for the console-like 4K/60fps targets that Valve is reportedly aiming for with the Steam Machine. Sure, it works great with the docked Deck, but we all know that it's the Steam Machine that preparations are currently being made for. The per-display scaling feature also helps, since you can now set independent scale factors for different monitors, which fixes a common issue where UI elements look massive or tiny when switching from handheld to TV.

With many Linux distros moving to Wayland and leaving X11 behind as their display server protocol, SteamOS is now doing the same. Both SteamOS and KDE Plasma, its desktop environment, are now being updated to support Wayland, and that's where the HDR, VRR, and rotated display support comes from. With that, what we can say for certain is that we're closer than ever to seeing the Steam Machine, and real-world preparations are being put in place through SteamOS for the same.

Steam Deck OLED
Dimensions
11.7 x 4.6 x 1.9 inches (298mm x 117mm x 49mm)
Brand
Valve
Weight
1.41 pounds (640 grams)
Chipset
Custom AMD Zen 2 APU (4 cores/8 threads, up to 3.5GHz boost)
RAM
16GB LPDDR5 6400MT/s
Storage
512GB or 1TB NVMe SSD, microSD card slot

Valve's upgraded Steam Deck features a larger OLED display with HDR support, faster Wi-Fi, and a bigger battery. Plus, this new model is slightly lighter, has slightly faster RAM, and it comes with storage up to 1TB. If you're looking for the ultimate Steam Deck, this is the version for you.

See at Steam
I just don't care about the Steam Deck 2 anymore

The Steam Deck's sequel won't revolutionize the industry in the same way the original did.

48
By  Jacob Roach

This isn't a shift you can feel until you look beyond the Deck

The Steam Deck is now refined and complete, but everything around it is just getting started.

SteamOS 3.8 is certainly a turning point, but not in the way you'd expect when you install it on your Steam Deck. It doesn't redefine the device in any sense of the word, but that's because it doesn't really need to, either. Instead, it redefines what SteamOS is allowed to be.

The Steam Deck is now more stable and complete than ever, because it has already arrived at its destination. Everything around it, however, is just getting started. SteamOS has now refined its original device, and now, it's enabling many others, and that might end up being the most important change Valve has made yet.

item.title: I replaced VLC with this free Windows 11 media player and it's absolutely beautiful
item.itunes:title: I replaced VLC with this free Windows 11 media player and it's absolutely beautiful
item.itunes:author: XDA[RSS+]
item.itunes:summary:
VLC is a juggernaut in the media player space, and it's been that way for years. Even dating back to the early days of its existence, I remember getting VLC and it being the de facto media player to replace the built-in Windows solution. And 16 years later, it's as popular as ever.

But one thing about VLC is getting a little long in the tooth, and that's the UI. Using it on Windows 11 today feels a little jarring thanks to stark contrast between its UI and that of other Windows apps, as well as the OS itself. Screenbox is the perfect solution for this; it's a free media player based on VLC's technology, but with a modern UI that fits perfectly into Windows 11.

Screenbox is free and open-source

And it works with basically everything

Right off the bat, it's great when software is free and open-source, and Screenbox checks both of those criteria. You can find the app on GitHub, though it can also be installed from the Microsoft Store or the official GitHub repository. It's specifically aimed at Windows, though, so no luck for Linux or macOS users with this one.

Screenbox is also based on LibVLCSharp, which is a video and audio API coming straight from VideoLAN, the team that develops VLC. This API allows other developers to implement all the same playback features and format compatibility that VLC has, making it easier for other developers to craft an app that's just as good.

What that means is Screenbox supports essentially every audio and video format under the sun, so whatever you want to watch on your computer, it works here. Not only that, but it also supports playing videos over the internet, just as VLC does. Don't expect something like YouTube to work reliably (VLC usually has trouble, too), but if a URL grants direct access to a video file, then Screenbox can parse it and play it directly inside its own window.

5 open-source media players that put VLC to shame

It's time to move on from VLC

28
By  Sumukh Rao

It looks great

Sleek and modern in all the right ways

As much as we can criticize Microsoft's decisions with Windows 11, the general design language the company has been using is, in my opinion, pretty beautiful and has fantastic potential. It's always a shame when developers, many times including the ones at Microsoft, don't follow the guidelines put out by the company, as it would create a cohesive experience that looks great.

VLC is one of many apps that hasn't cared to look particularly modern these days. The buttons have been cleaned up a bit and they look cleaner, but the current UI is still very much reminiscent of the first release, and it comes with some downsides, like a good chunk of space being taken by the UI until you go into fullscreen mode.

Screenbox addresses all of this and looks absolutely fantastic. The app makes full use of the translucent Mica material in Windows 11, offering just a hint of the background under certain surfaces, and when media is playing, all the playback controls are designed to be as non-intrusive as possible. There isn't a big opaque bar that obscures everything or forces video to be resized; each individual control floats above the video so you still have a good look at what's underneath. Buttons use wireframe icons, too, so they cover less of the video.

Another benefit of making use of native Windows APIs is support for a compact overlay window. Essentially, you can minimize screenbox into a tiny floating window what's always pinned on top of other apps, so you can keep tabs on a video or have easy access to playback controls without having to press Alt + Tab all the time to switch windows.

6 open-source alternatives to Windows Media Player that are probably better than it

The community does what Microsoft can't

14
By  João Carrasqueira

It also has library management features

More than just a player

Another notable benefit of Screenbox over VLC is that it's not just a media player in the purest sense of the word. VLC may be great for playing media, but that's all it really does. It doesn't have a way to organize and browse your library or anything of the kind.

Screenbox takes some cues from Microsoft's own Media Player app, with a full-fledged UI including a home page where you can see recently played files, as well as tabs for music and videos, so you can browse your music and media library with a UI that makes sense for both kinds of media. Music is displayed in a single view by default and organized alphabetically, though you can also switch to an album or artist view to see music from specific artists. Of course, you can also create playlists, which have a dedicated section of their own.

For videos, content is generally shown in a folder structure, similar to browsing your files in a file manager. You can opt for a collective view of all your videos from folders included in your library, though.

Of course, for both videos and music, you can also tweak where Screenbox scans for your items, so you have easier access to all your media in one place. You can also connect to media servers to access remote media from your other devices, though I'm not entirely sure how this works, and that's definitely a me problem.

9 hidden features in VLC you might not know about

VLC is one of the better media players around but how many of these features do you already use?

By  Joe Rice-Jones

Will VLC ever look like this?

Version 4.0 is still coming

The team at VLC has been working on version 4.0 of the music player for quite some time now, and nightly builds are available to try. A quick look at them reveals that the UI for VLC is going to get a radical revamp that learns quite a bit from Screenbox, though it still doesn't feature easy-to-use library management features. The UI as a whole does look a lot cleaner, though, which is a step in the right direction.

Subscribe to the newsletter for better Windows app picks

Curate a better media experience by subscribing to the newsletter, get focused coverage of modern Windows media players, UI-driven app recommendations, library and playback tips, and thoughtful comparisons to help you choose the right tool.
By subscribing, you agree to receive newsletter and marketing emails, and accept our Terms of Use and Privacy Policy. You can unsubscribe anytime.

The question is when it will be released. I remember trying this months, if not over a year ago, and it was very unstable, but things today still don't look amazing. I downloaded the latest build at the time of writing, and just loading a 4-second-long video seemed to take a couple of seconds.

In the meantime, Screenbox may just be the best way to play your media on Windows. And if you're on macOS, you might want to check out IINA, another beautiful media player with extremely robust support for different kinds of media. One of my colleagues has talked about it before, and I recommend checking out that article if you're curious.

Screenbox
See at Microsoft Store See at Github

What that means is Screenbox supports essentially every audio and video format under the sun, so whatever you want to watch on your computer, it works here. Not only that, but it also supports playing videos over the internet, just as VLC does. Don't expect something like YouTube to work reliably (VLC usually has trouble, too), but if a URL grants direct access to a video file, then Screenbox can parse it and play it directly inside its own window.

5 open-source media players that put VLC to shame

It's time to move on from VLC

28
By  Sumukh Rao

It looks great

Sleek and modern in all the right ways

As much as we can criticize Microsoft's decisions with Windows 11, the general design language the company has been using is, in my opinion, pretty beautiful and has fantastic potential. It's always a shame when developers, many times including the ones at Microsoft, don't follow the guidelines put out by the company, as it would create a cohesive experience that looks great.

VLC is one of many apps that hasn't cared to look particularly modern these days. The buttons have been cleaned up a bit and they look cleaner, but the current UI is still very much reminiscent of the first release, and it comes with some downsides, like a good chunk of space being taken by the UI until you go into fullscreen mode.

Screenbox addresses all of this and looks absolutely fantastic. The app makes full use of the translucent Mica material in Windows 11, offering just a hint of the background under certain surfaces, and when media is playing, all the playback controls are designed to be as non-intrusive as possible. There isn't a big opaque bar that obscures everything or forces video to be resized; each individual control floats above the video so you still have a good look at what's underneath. Buttons use wireframe icons, too, so they cover less of the video.

Another benefit of making use of native Windows APIs is support for a compact overlay window. Essentially, you can minimize screenbox into a tiny floating window what's always pinned on top of other apps, so you can keep tabs on a video or have easy access to playback controls without having to press Alt + Tab all the time to switch windows.

6 open-source alternatives to Windows Media Player that are probably better than it

The community does what Microsoft can't

14
By  João Carrasqueira

It also has library management features

More than just a player

Another notable benefit of Screenbox over VLC is that it's not just a media player in the purest sense of the word. VLC may be great for playing media, but that's all it really does. It doesn't have a way to organize and browse your library or anything of the kind.

Screenbox takes some cues from Microsoft's own Media Player app, with a full-fledged UI including a home page where you can see recently played files, as well as tabs for music and videos, so you can browse your music and media library with a UI that makes sense for both kinds of media. Music is displayed in a single view by default and organized alphabetically, though you can also switch to an album or artist view to see music from specific artists. Of course, you can also create playlists, which have a dedicated section of their own.

For videos, content is generally shown in a folder structure, similar to browsing your files in a file manager. You can opt for a collective view of all your videos from folders included in your library, though.

Of course, for both videos and music, you can also tweak where Screenbox scans for your items, so you have easier access to all your media in one place. You can also connect to media servers to access remote media from your other devices, though I'm not entirely sure how this works, and that's definitely a me problem.

9 hidden features in VLC you might not know about

VLC is one of the better media players around but how many of these features do you already use?

By  Joe Rice-Jones

Will VLC ever look like this?

Version 4.0 is still coming

The team at VLC has been working on version 4.0 of the music player for quite some time now, and nightly builds are available to try. A quick look at them reveals that the UI for VLC is going to get a radical revamp that learns quite a bit from Screenbox, though it still doesn't feature easy-to-use library management features. The UI as a whole does look a lot cleaner, though, which is a step in the right direction.

Subscribe to the newsletter for better Windows app picks

Curate a better media experience by subscribing to the newsletter, get focused coverage of modern Windows media players, UI-driven app recommendations, library and playback tips, and thoughtful comparisons to help you choose the right tool.
By subscribing, you agree to receive newsletter and marketing emails, and accept our Terms of Use and Privacy Policy. You can unsubscribe anytime.

The question is when it will be released. I remember trying this months, if not over a year ago, and it was very unstable, but things today still don't look amazing. I downloaded the latest build at the time of writing, and just loading a 4-second-long video seemed to take a couple of seconds.

In the meantime, Screenbox may just be the best way to play your media on Windows. And if you're on macOS, you might want to check out IINA, another beautiful media player with extremely robust support for different kinds of media. One of my colleagues has talked about it before, and I recommend checking out that article if you're curious.

Screenbox
See at Microsoft Store See at Github
item.content:encoded:
VLC is a juggernaut in the media player space, and it's been that way for years. Even dating back to the early days of its existence, I remember getting VLC and it being the de facto media player to replace the built-in Windows solution. And 16 years later, it's as popular as ever.

But one thing about VLC is getting a little long in the tooth, and that's the UI. Using it on Windows 11 today feels a little jarring thanks to stark contrast between its UI and that of other Windows apps, as well as the OS itself. Screenbox is the perfect solution for this; it's a free media player based on VLC's technology, but with a modern UI that fits perfectly into Windows 11.

Screenbox is free and open-source

And it works with basically everything

Right off the bat, it's great when software is free and open-source, and Screenbox checks both of those criteria. You can find the app on GitHub, though it can also be installed from the Microsoft Store or the official GitHub repository. It's specifically aimed at Windows, though, so no luck for Linux or macOS users with this one.

Screenbox is also based on LibVLCSharp, which is a video and audio API coming straight from VideoLAN, the team that develops VLC. This API allows other developers to implement all the same playback features and format compatibility that VLC has, making it easier for other developers to craft an app that's just as good.

What that means is Screenbox supports essentially every audio and video format under the sun, so whatever you want to watch on your computer, it works here. Not only that, but it also supports playing videos over the internet, just as VLC does. Don't expect something like YouTube to work reliably (VLC usually has trouble, too), but if a URL grants direct access to a video file, then Screenbox can parse it and play it directly inside its own window.

5 open-source media players that put VLC to shame

It's time to move on from VLC

28
By  Sumukh Rao

9 hidden features in VLC you might not know about

Subscribe to the newsletter for better Windows app picks

The question is when it will be released. I remember trying this months, if not over a year ago, and it was very unstable, but things today still don't look amazing. I downloaded the latest build at the time of writing, and just loading a 4-second-long video seemed to take a couple of seconds.

See at Microsoft Store See at Github

item.title: Plex gets a fresh look on Fire TV — and a new paywall
item.itunes:title: Plex gets a fresh look on Fire TV — and a new paywall
item.itunes:author: XDA[RSS+]
item.itunes:summary:
Summary

  • Plex has released a redesigned Fire TV app with a modern, Netflix-like UI and improved navigation.
  • Plex now restricts remote streaming on Roku: server owners need a Plex Pass, or visitors need a Remote Watch Pass.
  • Many users are upset, and some are migrating to Jellyfin.

Plex has finally released its redesigned app for Amazon's Fire TV platform, bringing a more refined, modern UI to the self-hosting streaming app, alongside remote-streaming restrictions that will likely frustrate many users (via How-To Geek).

On other platforms, Plex has ditched the ability to stream content remotely for free. To watch content hosted by a friend or family member, you need to either pay for a Plex Pass ($7/month, $70/year, or $250/lifetime) if you own the server, or buy a Remote Watch Pass ($2/month or $20/year).

Plex announced this subscription requirement back in November 2025, and restrictions arrived on iOS and Android first, followed by Roku and desktop. The paid requirement now extends to the smart TV version of the app across LG, Samsung, and Vizio TVs, though not all platforms have been updated yet. Plex says its smart TV app lineup will be updated with its new app UI by the end of 2026.

I didn’t realize my Plex Pass lets other users stream remotely from my server for free

A Remote Watch Pass isn't required.

By  Patrick O'Rourke

Plex's new user interface looks great

If you already have a Plex Pass, remote watching is free

Overall, the new Plex UI is a notable step above the previous version of the app. It features faster, more streamlined navigation and better use of space. It sort of looks like your own self-hosted version of Netflix.

Still, no one likes to pay for something that was once free, so I understand the criticism surrounding this change and why many people have jumped ship for Jellyfin, a free, open-source alternative to Plex. However, if you're a Plex Pass subscriber, anyone you add to your account can continue to stream your content remotely for free. Plex doesn't hide this loop hope, but it's not really upfront about it either, so it's worth highlighting.

For what it's worth, Plex says it's using the extra funds from this policy shift to add new features and support its existing platform, which is always a welcome thing to hear. Plex's Fire TV update hasn't landed on my Fire TV Stick 4K Max yet. To check whether you have access to the update, open the Plex app, press the three-line button on your Fire TV remote, then select More Info. If the update has landed on your device, you'll see the option to download it.

Plex
OS
Windows, macOS, Linux
Individual pricing
Free, $1.99/month, or $6.99/monyh
See available plans at Plex
3 key features where Plex still has the edge over Jellyfin — for now

Jellyfin is starting to catch up with the self-hosting giant, though.

12
By  Patrick O'Rourke

item.title: Stop blaming your ISP: most home networks are failing you in ways speed won't fix
item.itunes:title: Stop blaming your ISP: most home networks are failing you in ways speed won't fix
item.itunes:author: XDA[RSS+]
item.itunes:summary:
As many tech enthusiasts have experienced first hand, getting the internet speed you pay for is hardly a straightforward affair. You pay for a 1Gbps package, but the numbers you're seeing don't match. Or, maybe the speed tests are checking out, but streaming still stutters, gaming latency spikes constantly, and file transfers are sluggish. Either way, the experience is frustrating, and while it might be easy to reach for your phone and call your ISP to complain or ask for a better package, it's rarely the limiting factor. The bottleneck is almost always due to something inside your home: an aging or poorly placed router, congested bands, or outdated wiring within your walls.

5 reasons your internet connection might be slower than advertised

If your internet is slower than it should be, here's what may be causing it and some things you should check to fix it.

4
By  Adam Conway

Most households aren't going to saturate high-speed packages

Anything above gigabit just isn't going to get fully utilized

Before blaming the ISP, it's worth understanding what your household is actually consuming. Even in a busy home with a dozen or more connected devices, simultaneous peak bandwidth demand rarely approaches the ceiling of a mid-tier internet plan. Streaming 4K from a major platform consumes somewhere in the range of 15 to 25Mbps per stream. A household running three or four simultaneous 4K streams is pulling somewhere around 75 to 100Mbps, which is well within the capacity of a 200Mbps plan, let alone a gigabit one.

If you run a home lab, the majority of network traffic used in that context is all internal traffic, which doesn't touch your WAN unless you've exposed services to the internet. Transferring files to a NAS, streaming locally from a media server, casting from a phone to a TV, and running any kind of other internal service won't affect your internet performance, so the speed of the connection entering your home doesn't matter either.

When it comes to things like latency and jitter, the conversation gets a little more complicated, but as it relates to internet speed, faster doesn't mean better. Latency is delay between a request and response, and jitter is the variance in that delay over time. These are the numbers that determine whether your online gaming session is smooth or full of stutters, whether your video call drops frames, whether your VoIP audio breaks up. None of them are meaningfully improved by doubling your download speed.

5 router settings most people never touch that fixed my network problems

Lesser-known fixes for well-known problems

10
By  Chandraveer Mathur

Your hardware is probably failing you

Bad placement or hardware in need of replacement

Even among technically aware users, home networking hardware tends to get neglected in a way that other PC components don't. Routers sit in corners for years without being replaced, and ISP-provided gateway units are often underpowered combo devices that handle modem and router tasks simultaneously, which can also cause issues.

Beyond the wireless standards themselves, which have been improving a ton with each passing version, the physical deployment matters an enormous amount and is routinely neglected. A single router positioned in a corner of the house, tucked behind furniture or inside a cabinet, is doing its worst work from its worst possible location. Simply moving the router to a more centralized location can work wonders. Co-channel interference can degrade performance significantly as well, and that also has nothing to do with how much speed you pay for.

When it comes to wired connectivity, if you're utilizing any part of your home's internal wiring for an Ethernet connection, there's a good chance that could be the culprit. Many houses that have Ethernet runs in the walls are wired with Cat5 or older Cat5e cable. Under ideal conditions, Cat5e supports up to 1Gbps, but real-world performance on longer runs or degraded cable can fall well short of that ceiling. Cat5 offers even less headroom. For users who have upgraded to multi-gigabit WAN connections, or who want to move large volumes of data quickly between wired devices, this existing infrastructure becomes a silent bottleneck. The cable in the wall that was installed ten or fifteen years ago was not specced for the workloads being asked of it today. Even if the cable is rated for speeds above gigabit, there's a good chance your hardware only has gigabit ports, even with 2.5 GbE becoming the norm on many devices.

Multi-gigabit routers are pointless without this one upgrade

Take full advantage of your speeds with this addition

16
By  Jasmine Mannan

Bandwidth from the ISP still matters

You can only do so much

Sometimes it really is the case that the speeds you're seeing aren't due to your own hardware. A household with several people working from home simultaneously, each on video calls that run for hours, has genuine upload requirements that a lower-tier asymmetric plan just can't support comfortably. In those cases, getting a higher package will definitely help remedy things.

Heavy download workloads are less commonly a genuine bottleneck at mid-tier plan speeds, but they exist. A household with multiple users simultaneously pulling large game updates, streaming 4K from several different sources, and running other bandwidth-intensive tasks concurrently can approach or exceed the capacity of a 100 to 200Mbps plan. In these cases, upgrading makes sense.

5 router settings most people never touch that fixed my network problems

Lesser-known fixes for well-known problems

10
By  Chandraveer Mathur

You're right to be wary of your ISP, but it's worth checking your own setup before picking up the phone

The reflex to treat slow or unreliable home networking as an ISP problem is deeply ingrained, and the ISPs have done nothing to discourage it. Faster plans are easy to sell and easy to understand, which runs opposite to internal networking. But for the overwhelming majority of home users, including technically demanding ones, the meaningful gains are inside the house—in the router, the access points, the cabling, the configuration, and the architecture of the network itself. Getting those things right should always come before writing a larger check to the ISP.

Or, maybe the speed tests are checking out, but streaming still stutters, gaming latency spikes constantly, and file transfers are sluggish. Either way, the experience is frustrating, and while it might be easy to reach for your phone and call your ISP to complain or ask for a better package, it's rarely the limiting factor. The bottleneck is almost always due to something inside your home: an aging or poorly placed router, congested bands, or outdated wiring within your walls.

5 reasons your internet connection might be slower than advertised

If your internet is slower than it should be, here's what may be causing it and some things you should check to fix it.

4
By  Adam Conway

Most households aren't going to saturate high-speed packages

Anything above gigabit just isn't going to get fully utilized

Before blaming the ISP, it's worth understanding what your household is actually consuming. Even in a busy home with a dozen or more connected devices, simultaneous peak bandwidth demand rarely approaches the ceiling of a mid-tier internet plan. Streaming 4K from a major platform consumes somewhere in the range of 15 to 25Mbps per stream. A household running three or four simultaneous 4K streams is pulling somewhere around 75 to 100Mbps, which is well within the capacity of a 200Mbps plan, let alone a gigabit one.

If you run a home lab, the majority of network traffic used in that context is all internal traffic, which doesn't touch your WAN unless you've exposed services to the internet. Transferring files to a NAS, streaming locally from a media server, casting from a phone to a TV, and running any kind of other internal service won't affect your internet performance, so the speed of the connection entering your home doesn't matter either.

When it comes to things like latency and jitter, the conversation gets a little more complicated, but as it relates to internet speed, faster doesn't mean better. Latency is delay between a request and response, and jitter is the variance in that delay over time. These are the numbers that determine whether your online gaming session is smooth or full of stutters, whether your video call drops frames, whether your VoIP audio breaks up. None of them are meaningfully improved by doubling your download speed.

5 router settings most people never touch that fixed my network problems

Lesser-known fixes for well-known problems

10
By  Chandraveer Mathur

Your hardware is probably failing you

Bad placement or hardware in need of replacement

Even among technically aware users, home networking hardware tends to get neglected in a way that other PC components don't. Routers sit in corners for years without being replaced, and ISP-provided gateway units are often underpowered combo devices that handle modem and router tasks simultaneously, which can also cause issues.

Beyond the wireless standards themselves, which have been improving a ton with each passing version, the physical deployment matters an enormous amount and is routinely neglected. A single router positioned in a corner of the house, tucked behind furniture or inside a cabinet, is doing its worst work from its worst possible location. Simply moving the router to a more centralized location can work wonders. Co-channel interference can degrade performance significantly as well, and that also has nothing to do with how much speed you pay for.

When it comes to wired connectivity, if you're utilizing any part of your home's internal wiring for an Ethernet connection, there's a good chance that could be the culprit. Many houses that have Ethernet runs in the walls are wired with Cat5 or older Cat5e cable. Under ideal conditions, Cat5e supports up to 1Gbps, but real-world performance on longer runs or degraded cable can fall well short of that ceiling. Cat5 offers even less headroom. For users who have upgraded to multi-gigabit WAN connections, or who want to move large volumes of data quickly between wired devices, this existing infrastructure becomes a silent bottleneck. The cable in the wall that was installed ten or fifteen years ago was not specced for the workloads being asked of it today. Even if the cable is rated for speeds above gigabit, there's a good chance your hardware only has gigabit ports, even with 2.5 GbE becoming the norm on many devices.

Multi-gigabit routers are pointless without this one upgrade

Take full advantage of your speeds with this addition

16
By  Jasmine Mannan

Bandwidth from the ISP still matters

You can only do so much

Sometimes it really is the case that the speeds you're seeing aren't due to your own hardware. A household with several people working from home simultaneously, each on video calls that run for hours, has genuine upload requirements that a lower-tier asymmetric plan just can't support comfortably. In those cases, getting a higher package will definitely help remedy things.

Heavy download workloads are less commonly a genuine bottleneck at mid-tier plan speeds, but they exist. A household with multiple users simultaneously pulling large game updates, streaming 4K from several different sources, and running other bandwidth-intensive tasks concurrently can approach or exceed the capacity of a 100 to 200Mbps plan. In these cases, upgrading makes sense.

5 router settings most people never touch that fixed my network problems

Lesser-known fixes for well-known problems

10
By  Chandraveer Mathur

You're right to be wary of your ISP, but it's worth checking your own setup before picking up the phone

The reflex to treat slow or unreliable home networking as an ISP problem is deeply ingrained, and the ISPs have done nothing to discourage it. Faster plans are easy to sell and easy to understand, which runs opposite to internal networking. But for the overwhelming majority of home users, including technically demanding ones, the meaningful gains are inside the house—in the router, the access points, the cabling, the configuration, and the architecture of the network itself. Getting those things right should always come before writing a larger check to the ISP.

item.title: The Flatpak versus native packages debate finally made sense when I stopped looking for a winner
item.itunes:title: The Flatpak versus native packages debate finally made sense when I stopped looking for a winner
item.itunes:author: XDA[RSS+]
item.itunes:summary:
For a long time, I treated the Flatpak-versus-native package debate like one of those Linux arguments that never really end and never really change anything. I knew the usual talking points, and I’d heard them from every angle. Flatpaks were supposed to be more convenient, native packages were supposed to be cleaner, and everyone seemed convinced their camp had already won. The longer I used Linux, though, the less that tidy split matched what I was actually doing on my own system.

Once I stopped trying to crown a universal winner, the whole subject became much easier to navigate.

What changed my mind wasn’t one dramatic failure or one magical app install that fixed everything overnight. It was the slow realization that I was using different kinds of software for different reasons, but pretending they all belonged in the same bucket. Some apps needed to stay close to the system and feel tightly integrated. Others just needed to work, stay current, and avoid dragging me into dependency drama. Once I started looking at packages through that lens, the Flatpak-versus-native debate stopped feeling ideological and started feeling practical.

Linux distros can't agree on how to install apps, but Distrobox makes that problem disappear

Why limit yourself?

By  João Carrasqueira

I stopped expecting one packaging format to do everything

Different apps deserve different installation priorities and tradeoffs

One of the biggest mistakes I made was assuming every Linux app should be judged by the same standards. That sounds sensible at first, but it falls apart pretty quickly in real use. A text editor, a media player, a system monitor, and a GPU utility don’t all need the same level of system access or integration. Treating them as if they do just creates frustration where there doesn’t need to be any.

Flatpak started making more sense to me when I stopped asking whether it was better in the abstract and started asking whether it was better for a specific app. For a cross-platform app that updates often, Flatpak can feel like a relief. I don’t have to wonder whether my distro’s repositories are lagging behind or whether some third-party repo will become a maintenance chore six months from now. I can install the app, use it, and move on with my life.

Native packages still matter a lot, though, especially for software that feels like part of the operating system itself. If I’m installing lower-level tools, terminal utilities, drivers, codecs, or anything that depends heavily on system libraries and tight integration, I still want the native package whenever possible. That route usually feels more predictable, and it tends to respect the distro’s own logic. Once I stopped trying to crown a universal winner, the whole subject became much easier to navigate.

Convenience started mattering more than I wanted to admit

The cleanest option is not always the best one

Linux users love the idea of a clean, elegant system, and I’m definitely not immune to that. There’s a real satisfaction in knowing where software comes from, how it fits into the distro, and what your package manager is doing behind the scenes. Native packages scratch that itch beautifully when everything lines up. The problem is that real life on Linux doesn’t always reward that kind of purity.

Sometimes I just want the latest version of an app without adding a PPA, enabling a COPR, hunting down an AUR package, or hoping my distro catches up eventually. That’s where Flatpak stopped feeling like a compromise and started feeling like a very smart default for certain categories of software. GUI apps, especially those that move quickly or rely heavily on upstream development, are often easier to live with in Flatpak form. I spend less time maintaining them, and that counts for a lot.

That shift changed how I thought about convenience in general. I used to treat convenience as something slightly suspect, as if I were cutting corners by taking the easier path. Now I see it as part of system design. A package format that reduces friction, lowers the odds of breaking something, and keeps software current is doing real work for me. Linux doesn’t get less serious just because I don’t want to micromanage every desktop app.

There are still reasons native packages feel more honest

System integration and overhead still matter quite a bit

None of that means Flatpak wins every round, because it absolutely doesn’t. There are still times when native packages feel better, lighter, and more appropriate for the job. Launch times can be snappier, theming can be more consistent, and the whole app can feel a little less boxed in. When something is deeply tied to the desktop or the hardware, native packaging often still feels like the most natural fit.

There’s also the storage question, and I don’t think Linux users are wrong to care about it. Flatpaks can be heavier, and the overhead is noticeable if you install many of them. Shared runtimes help, but they don’t make the concern disappear. If someone prefers native packages because they want a leaner system with fewer duplicated components, that’s a perfectly reasonable position and not just forum drama dressed up as principle.

I also understand why some people simply trust distro maintainers more than app vendors or platform-specific ecosystems. Native packages feel grounded in the distro’s broader design, and that has value. They’re part of a coherent whole rather than something layered on top of it. If your Linux setup is built around stability, consistency, and close system alignment, native packages can still feel like the more honest answer.

What mattered most was changing my expectations first

I finally matched package formats to actual app roles

Even with those drawbacks, I don’t think the lesson here is that Flatpak is only a fallback for people who don’t know better. For me, the real breakthrough was realizing that I had been assigning the wrong job to the wrong tool. I kept expecting native packages to deliver maximum freshness with minimal hassle, and I kept expecting Flatpaks to behave exactly like traditional system packages. Neither format was failing me as much as I was misusing both.

Once I split my apps into rough categories, things clicked almost immediately. Core system tools, drivers, utilities, and deeply integrated software stayed native. Everyday desktop apps, creative tools, chat clients, note-taking apps, and other fast-moving GUI software became much stronger candidates for Flatpak. That approach didn’t just tidy up my app management. It made Linux feel more deliberate and less argumentative.

I eventually landed on a pretty simple rule: if an app mostly needs to launch, stay updated, and keep its own mess contained, Flatpak is usually the better fit. If it needs deep system access, tight desktop integration, or lives closer to the OS's plumbing, I’d rather trust the distro’s native repo. It’s not perfect, but it gets me to the right answer more often than not. The table below should provide you with a good idea of what to rely on Flatpak to manage, and what to keep under the domain of your distro's native package manager.

App type

Best choice

Chat, note-taking, and other cross-platform desktop apps

Flatpak

Creative apps and most third-party GUI tools

Flatpak

Web browsers and media apps

Usually Flatpak

Terminal tools and developer utilities

Native repo

Desktop environment apps and system utilities

Native repo

Drivers, codecs, and firmware-related packages

Native repo

This revelation also helped me appreciate that Linux doesn’t have to imitate a single-platform ecosystem to be usable. Part of its strength is that it gives me choices, even if those choices sometimes come with a little extra thinking. The trick is not to pretend that every choice needs to be permanent or universal. When I stopped asking which package format was the correct one and started asking which one fit the app in front of me, I ended up with a system that felt more flexible and less fragile.

Why this packaging debate changed how I use Linux

The biggest shift wasn’t technical. It was mental. I used to think package formats reflected a philosophy I had to commit to, almost as if I were choosing what kind of Linux user I wanted to be. Now I see them more like layers in a toolbox, each useful in the right situation and annoying in the wrong one. That sounds less romantic, maybe, but it’s a lot more useful when you actually have work to do.

Flatpak didn’t replace native packages for me, and native packages didn’t push Flatpak out of the picture either. What changed is that I stopped trying to force one answer onto every app I install. Linux got easier once I accepted that different software deserves different treatment. That’s what really made me rethink how I use Linux apps, and honestly, I wish I’d let myself figure that out sooner.

item.title: I built an offline research system, and cloud AI doesn't feel necessary anymore
item.itunes:title: I built an offline research system, and cloud AI doesn't feel necessary anymore
item.itunes:author: XDA[RSS+]
item.itunes:summary:
Like most people in the AI era, the majority of my research has become dependent on cloud-based tools. Perplexity for quick answers, ChatGPT for brainstorming, Gemini for web content, and so on. None of them work offline, none of them know what the others are doing, and at least two of them have cost me a monthly subscription. All of this can feel a little overkill at times just for collecting information.

I wanted to create a setup that I could access anytime and where everything could easily connect but without requiring me to go online. Once I started focusing on the idea of an “offline system”, everything else also started falling into place. These tools not only work without a connection, but they also give me more control over how or if I go online, and some of them integrate perfectly with each other…

The problem with using cloud AI for research

Cloud tools are powerful, but they don’t come without drawbacks

I’m not against using cloud-based AI tools for research; they’re popular for a reason. They’re very fast thanks to enterprise-level GPUs, and they’re super easy to jump into because you just open them and use them; there’s usually not much to configure. Whether I’m summarizing a long document, collecting information, extracting key points, or trying to think through an idea, cloud-based tools almost always make that process feel effortless.

There are, however, downsides to relying on them for research. Cloud AI requires transmitting sensitive data to third-party servers, which raises concerns about things like potential unauthorized access and data residency issues, especially concerning health and financial information. This is why I always feel a little uncertain diving into specific topics using cloud chatbots or AI browsers.

There’s also the issue of bias and integrity. Cloud tools are largely turned to be agreeable and optimized for user satisfaction, which means they’re more likely to validate whatever you bring to them. This can make them feel better to use, but it’s not really what you want from a research tool. And of course, you also can’t use them offline.

Offline research doesn’t have to be a project

You just need one or two reliable tools…

The core part of my offline system is simply my local LLM, and that’s probably going to be the most dependable type of tool you can use for offline research. I run my model in LM Studio, but there are tons of other model runners that might be better suited to your use cases. The runner honestly matters less than the model you load into it - a capable model with a large and diverse training set is what will actually determine how useful the setup is for research.

I stuck with gpt-oss because it’s an open-weight model from OpenAI, so you’re getting the same training quality, just without the cloud and internet dependency. It’s a 20B model that punches well above its weight, and OpenAI specifically trained it on general knowledge alongside STEM, which makes it a natural fit for my use cases. With this model, it won’t give me anything beyond mid-2024, but that covers the vast majority of what I actually research. If I do need something current, I just enable the Brave Search MCP plugin to pull real-time data. This does require a connection, but the point is that I get to make that choice.

There are options beyond local AI. For reference materials, a tool like Kiwix is worth having in your setup. It lets you download online content as local files using a .zim format, which can be opened and browsed offline, and it’s fully searchable. The text-only English version is storage-friendly enough and it’s actually faster than the live site. If you want something more dictionary-focused, then GoldenDict does the same thing but pulls from offline dictionary databases instead.

Turning offline research into a usable system

You need a hub for navigating the information

Once I have my research, it all lands in Obsidian. I choose Obsidian primarily because I hooked it up to LM Studio through the Copilot plugin, so I can query my local model from within my notes and knowledge base - still without needing a connection. You can also build pretty intricate and complex personal wikis and knowledge hubs in Obsidian using plugins; some will give you that Notion dashboard vibe. But I keep things simple with folders, subfolders, backlinking, and some formatting structure in the notes.

Obsidian makes it super easy to navigate large stacks of research with global search and advanced filters, and I just love its vault system for how it keeps my notes portable. There are other offline-first tools that will let you build personal knowledge systems the same way - some of my top recommendations would be Logseq and Joplin. Both are open-source, local-first, and handle large note collections well.

Offline-first doesn’t mean offline-only

This setup isn’t about going off-grid or making a statement about cloud AI, but more about having a research and information system on your own terms. A local LLM handles most of what I need, Kiwix covers information gaps, and Obsidian keeps everything in one, manageable place. Online and cloud-based tools still have their place, but it’s nice to have the option not to rely on them.
item.description:
Like most people in the AI era, the majority of my research has become dependent on cloud-based tools. Perplexity for quick answers, ChatGPT for brainstorming, Gemini for web content, and so on. None of them work offline, none of them know what the others are doing, and at least two of them have cost me a monthly subscription. All of this can feel a little overkill at times just for collecting information.

I wanted to create a setup that I could access anytime and where everything could easily connect but without requiring me to go online. Once I started focusing on the idea of an “offline system”, everything else also started falling into place. These tools not only work without a connection, but they also give me more control over how or if I go online, and some of them integrate perfectly with each other…

The problem with using cloud AI for research

Cloud tools are powerful, but they don’t come without drawbacks

I’m not against using cloud-based AI tools for research; they’re popular for a reason. They’re very fast thanks to enterprise-level GPUs, and they’re super easy to jump into because you just open them and use them; there’s usually not much to configure. Whether I’m summarizing a long document, collecting information, extracting key points, or trying to think through an idea, cloud-based tools almost always make that process feel effortless.

There are, however, downsides to relying on them for research. Cloud AI requires transmitting sensitive data to third-party servers, which raises concerns about things like potential unauthorized access and data residency issues, especially concerning health and financial information. This is why I always feel a little uncertain diving into specific topics using cloud chatbots or AI browsers.

There’s also the issue of bias and integrity. Cloud tools are largely turned to be agreeable and optimized for user satisfaction, which means they’re more likely to validate whatever you bring to them. This can make them feel better to use, but it’s not really what you want from a research tool. And of course, you also can’t use them offline.

Offline research doesn’t have to be a project

You just need one or two reliable tools…

The core part of my offline system is simply my local LLM, and that’s probably going to be the most dependable type of tool you can use for offline research. I run my model in LM Studio, but there are tons of other model runners that might be better suited to your use cases. The runner honestly matters less than the model you load into it - a capable model with a large and diverse training set is what will actually determine how useful the setup is for research.

I stuck with gpt-oss because it’s an open-weight model from OpenAI, so you’re getting the same training quality, just without the cloud and internet dependency. It’s a 20B model that punches well above its weight, and OpenAI specifically trained it on general knowledge alongside STEM, which makes it a natural fit for my use cases. With this model, it won’t give me anything beyond mid-2024, but that covers the vast majority of what I actually research. If I do need something current, I just enable the Brave Search MCP plugin to pull real-time data. This does require a connection, but the point is that I get to make that choice.

There are options beyond local AI. For reference materials, a tool like Kiwix is worth having in your setup. It lets you download online content as local files using a .zim format, which can be opened and browsed offline, and it’s fully searchable. The text-only English version is storage-friendly enough and it’s actually faster than the live site. If you want something more dictionary-focused, then GoldenDict does the same thing but pulls from offline dictionary databases instead.

Turning offline research into a usable system

You need a hub for navigating the information

Once I have my research, it all lands in Obsidian. I choose Obsidian primarily because I hooked it up to LM Studio through the Copilot plugin, so I can query my local model from within my notes and knowledge base - still without needing a connection. You can also build pretty intricate and complex personal wikis and knowledge hubs in Obsidian using plugins; some will give you that Notion dashboard vibe. But I keep things simple with folders, subfolders, backlinking, and some formatting structure in the notes.

Obsidian makes it super easy to navigate large stacks of research with global search and advanced filters, and I just love its vault system for how it keeps my notes portable. There are other offline-first tools that will let you build personal knowledge systems the same way - some of my top recommendations would be Logseq and Joplin. Both are open-source, local-first, and handle large note collections well.

Offline-first doesn’t mean offline-only

This setup isn’t about going off-grid or making a statement about cloud AI, but more about having a research and information system on your own terms. A local LLM handles most of what I need, Kiwix covers information gaps, and Obsidian keeps everything in one, manageable place. Online and cloud-based tools still have their place, but it’s nice to have the option not to rely on them.

item.title: 4 unique integrations you never thought of using with Home Assistant
item.itunes:title: 4 unique integrations you never thought of using with Home Assistant
item.itunes:author: XDA[RSS+]
item.itunes:summary:
On the surface, Home Assistant offers a neat control hub for managing smart devices and IoT gizmos. But once you start delving deep into HASS, you’ll notice that it’s an automation powerhouse that can drive your smart living space without requiring you to lift a finger. And that’s just the integrations natively available on Home Assistant.

Toss in the ingenious (and low-key unhinged) offerings on the integrations available on the Home Assistant Community Store, and HASS becomes a platform capable of linking practically every aspect of your tinkering ecosystem, be it your smart gadgets, microcontrollers, storage servers, and self-hosting workstations. I’ve been using HACS integrations extensively over the last couple of weeks, and these are some obscure repos that deserve more love from the community.

6 of the coolest HACS integrations for Home Assistant users

Level up your Home Assistant game with these neat HACS integrations

3
By  Ayush Pande

Mail and Packages

Receive package updates on your Home Assistant dashboard

Monitoring shipments from different logistics firms can be a bit of a hassle, especially since you’d have to switch between multiple websites to track your packages. That’s where the Mail and Packages integration comes in handy, as it lets you link different logistics accounts with your Home Assistant instance and turns typical shipments into sensor entities. That way, you can create custom dashboards featuring the transit status of your pending deliveries.

Likewise, you can use the entities generated by Mail and Packages in your automation workflows, be it turning on the porch lights or unlocking garage doors when a shipment is out for delivery. The Mail and Packages integration currently supports UPS MyChoice, USPS Informed Delivery, and FedEx Delivery Manager accounts, but you can also pair it with Amazon, DHL, Canada Post, and a bunch of other logistics platforms.

WashData

You’ll want to use smart plugs that can withstand exceedingly high loads

If you’ve got a normal washing machine and want to use it with your Home Assistant hub, pairing a typical smart plug with the WashData integration can be a decent solution. WashData monitors the energy consumption metrics recorded by the smart plug and uses them to estimate the washer cycle, operation mode, and remaining progress. The WashData integration also relies on pattern recognition to fine-tune the progress detection algorithms, so the more you use it, the more accurate its readings become.

Better yet, it even works with coffee machines, dryers, and electric vehicles, on top of including noise filtering provisions to avoid random boot energy spikes from messing up the algorithms. The only caveat with this integration is that you’ll have to use high-quality smart plugs that can withstand well over 16A (ideally 30A) of current. Otherwise, you might end up dealing with fire hazards in the worst-case scenario.

PowerCalc

To gauge the energy consumption metrics of your smart devices

I’ve been using smart plugs extensively ever since I bought some for my home lab, and it’s thanks to them that I realized my server nodes were pulling way more wattage than I’d anticipated. If you’re as much of a smart home enthusiast as I am, you’ll probably have a similar situation with your IoT gizmos. PowerCalc will be a great addition to your Home Assistant suite in this scenario, as it can calculate the energy consumption metrics for all the devices in your smart living space, including “dumb gadgets” that don’t feature built-in power sensors.

PowerCalc has dedicated energy profiles for hundreds of smart products, and it uses different strategies to estimate the power usage of your devices. For typical switches, it defaults to fixed wattage values, while lights involve dynamic calculation of their brightness, color, and temperature statistics. Better yet, PowerCalc meshes well with the Energy Dashboard integration, so you can import the virtual power sensors created by the former into the latter’s UI.

Watchman

For troubleshooting missing entities

Considering the sheer number of add-ons and integrations you can arm Home Assistant with, you might end up with hundreds of entities on your HASS control hub. Unfortunately, these entities can break because of faulty integrations, orphaned sensors, or just random disconnections. Besides making a mess of your lovely dashboards, these broken entities can cause your painstakingly crafted automations to fail. Worse still, finding faulty sensors can be a pain when you’ve got multiple entity failures.

Subscribe to the newsletter for top Home Assistant HACS picks

Discover deeper HASS insights: subscribe to the newsletter for curated HACS integration recommendations, practical configuration tips, troubleshooting help, and energy-monitoring guides that make managing and automating your smart home easier.
By subscribing, you agree to receive newsletter and marketing emails, and accept our Terms of Use and Privacy Policy. You can unsubscribe anytime.

Watchman makes troubleshooting these broken entities a cakewalk, as it scans all the devices mentioned in your YAML configs before checking whether they’re actually operational. Upon detecting missing entities, it flags them alongside all the automation chains and configuration files where they appear.

This lightweight program is a must-have for Home Assistant users like myself

This Windows-based companion app lets you monitor your PC and perform actions without opening Home Assistant.

4
By  Samir Makwana

Some more HACS integrations for hardcore tinkerers

Besides the obscure integrations I’ve mentioned so far, the Home Assistant Community Store has tons of other useful offerings. As a staunch member of the Proxmox faction, the PVE integration is one of my favorite repos on HACS, as it not only lets me monitor my home lab nodes from Home Assistant, but it also opens doors to some wacky automation projects combining my virtual guests with typical smart devices. Likewise, you’ll find integrations for TrueNAS, Unraid, OpenMediaVault, and other server platforms, while the unofficial Frigate repository makes pulling security camera footage and configuring motion detection automations a cinch. If you prefer using Vikunja, you can use its HACS integration to create notes on Home Assistant and store them on the to-do list builder. I’ve also found a bunch of pollen-detection tools, but sadly, none of them include readings for my backwater town.

Home Assistant
OS
Windows, macOS, Linux
iOS compatible
Yes
Android compatible
Yes
See at Apple App Store See at Google Play Store See at Home Assistant

item.title: The best $50 I ever spent on my PC didn't add a single frame to my games
item.itunes:title: The best $50 I ever spent on my PC didn't add a single frame to my games
item.itunes:author: XDA[RSS+]
item.itunes:summary:
We obsess over 1% low frame rates, overclocking RAM, and shaving milliseconds off latency; however, the reality is that you can have a $4,000 rig, but if you're hunching over like a gargoyle to see your screen, your user experience is actually terrible.

Something that made a major difference to my setup was a gas spring monitor arm. It might not be the flashiest of technology, but it's the only upgrade that physically changes how you interact with your PC for 8+ hours a day. PC enthusiasts can be obsessed with spec sheets; however, it might be time for you to pivot your perspective to optimize your ergonomics, which is often the most neglected part of the setup.

Stop ignoring these 5 non-hardware PC upgrades while chasing peak performance

Your desk setup is as important as the inside of your PC

3
By  Tanveer Singh

The standard stand just doesn't compare

A monitor arm is absolutely peak

Of course, every monitor you buy comes with a stand in the box; however, most included stands, even on many gaming monitors, are too low and don't allow you to adjust the height, leaving you with a static height. This isn't always the case, but it is more often than not. This forces the tech neck, where you are hunched over, to reach the optimal height of viewing your monitor. This can lead to a range of actual health problems and severely affect your posture in the long run, especially if you're working and gaming at your desk.

Another issue with the included stand is the desk footprint: standard stands are space hogs. They take up the most valuable real estate on your desk, the center, making it impossible to keep a notebook, tablet, or even a keyboard tucked away. Swapping over to a monitor arm frees up so much more space on your desk. By mounting your monitor, you're reclaiming 15–20% of your desk surface. Most budget-friendly arms even include built-in cable-routing channels that hide the rat's nest of cables living behind your screen right now.

There's also a lack of versatility with the standard stands that are included in the box. If you want to change the orientation of your monitor, it is usually a chore rather than the quick two-second adjustment it can be with a monitor arm. Having an arm also allows you to have a deep versus a shallow display. An arm lets you push the monitor farther back than a stand would, which is crucial for those using 32-inch panels or larger on a standard-depth desk.

Another thing you have to consider is the level of your eyes' revelation. There are so many reasons why ensuring your monitor is at the right level for your eyes is fundamental, especially if you're using your computer for long periods. When it comes to the rule of thirds, your eyes should naturally rest in the top third of your monitor so you're not slouching to feel level with the screen. When your neck is aligned, your stamina for gaming or coding increases. You aren't getting tired because of the game; you are likely to get tired because your trapezius muscles are doing a marathon.

North Bayou Dual Monitor Arm

If you're looking for a budget friendly monitor arm that still provides with excellent features, then look no futher than the North Bayou Dual Monitor Arm.

See at Amazon

The more relaxed you feel based on the ergonomics of your setup, the longer you will be able to sit at your desk comfortably. Another issue you might encounter is postural breathing. Lifting the monitor forces you to sit back, opening up your chest, and improving oxygen flow. While this might sound dramatic for long sessions, it's an absolute game-changer that can change how you feel whenever you are sitting at your desk.

Be sure to look out for these key specs

Some cheaper monitor arms might not be suitable based on your setup

When you're looking for a $50 monitor arm, don't let the low price tag fool you. There is a massive difference between a budget arm and a cheap arm. When you're shopping in the $50 range, there are a few non-negotiables that you need to check for on the box or in the specifications list.

Most $50 arms utilize a gas spring, which is a pressurized cylinder. This is the absolute gold standard for the majority of users because it allows for counter-balanced movement. You can adjust the tension with a hex key so that the monitor floats, and you should be able to move it with just one finger. It should stay exactly where you leave it.

Avoid opting for friction poles, which are basic metal tubes with a clamp, unless you never plan on moving your monitor at all. They require manual unscrewing just to change the height by an inch.

While it may seem obvious, ensure your monitor arm is compatible with your monitor. Most arms should support both 75 by 75 mm and 100 by 100 mm patterns, but this isn't always the case. Some monitors have the VESA holes recessed into a circular pocket. If the monitor's arm plate is too wide, it might not fit, so look for arms with spaces in the box to solve this issue.

Make subscribing count: Monitor-arm and ergonomics guides

Subscribe to the newsletter for hands-on monitor-arm reviews, practical ergonomics advice, compatibility and weight checklists, and desk-space strategies - clear coverage to help you pick and position the right arm.
By subscribing, you agree to receive newsletter and marketing emails, and accept our Terms of Use and Privacy Policy. You can unsubscribe anytime.

The weight capacity is a very fundamental part, too. Most budget arms are rated for 4.4–19.8 lb (about 2–9 kg). This is perfect for almost any 27 in or 32 in 16:9 monitor, but it can be a bit of a danger zone if you have a 34 in ultra-wide or a 49 in Odyssey G9. A $50 arm will likely droop at the tilt mechanism because it is too heavy. For those heavy hitters, you need to look for heavy-duty arms rated for 30 lb or more, which usually start at around $80–$100.

The mounting styles might be important for you, too. While most arms come with the hardware for both C-clamp and grommet mounting, make sure you double-check. The C-clamp is the most popular; it grips the edge of your desk. However, users who have glass desks or hollow honeycomb desks should use a reinforcement plate to prevent the arm from crushing the desk. If your desk has a cable hole or you're brave enough to drill one, then a grommet provides a much cleaner, integrated look and is physically more stable, too.

Frames aren't everything

Comfort can be more important than specs

While investing in a brand-new monitor arm won't yield a high number of frames in your benchmarks, you will feel significantly better physically after a four-hour session at your desk. We spend so much time optimizing the silicone; it's about time we optimize the human sitting in front of it, too. If you're sitting at your desk for multiple hours a day, why not make it as comfortable as possible?
item.description:
We obsess over 1% low frame rates, overclocking RAM, and shaving milliseconds off latency; however, the reality is that you can have a $4,000 rig, but if you're hunching over like a gargoyle to see your screen, your user experience is actually terrible.

Something that made a major difference to my setup was a gas spring monitor arm. It might not be the flashiest of technology, but it's the only upgrade that physically changes how you interact with your PC for 8+ hours a day. PC enthusiasts can be obsessed with spec sheets; however, it might be time for you to pivot your perspective to optimize your ergonomics, which is often the most neglected part of the setup.

Stop ignoring these 5 non-hardware PC upgrades while chasing peak performance

Your desk setup is as important as the inside of your PC

3
By  Tanveer Singh

The standard stand just doesn't compare

A monitor arm is absolutely peak

Of course, every monitor you buy comes with a stand in the box; however, most included stands, even on many gaming monitors, are too low and don't allow you to adjust the height, leaving you with a static height. This isn't always the case, but it is more often than not. This forces the tech neck, where you are hunched over, to reach the optimal height of viewing your monitor. This can lead to a range of actual health problems and severely affect your posture in the long run, especially if you're working and gaming at your desk.

Another issue with the included stand is the desk footprint: standard stands are space hogs. They take up the most valuable real estate on your desk, the center, making it impossible to keep a notebook, tablet, or even a keyboard tucked away. Swapping over to a monitor arm frees up so much more space on your desk. By mounting your monitor, you're reclaiming 15–20% of your desk surface. Most budget-friendly arms even include built-in cable-routing channels that hide the rat's nest of cables living behind your screen right now.

There's also a lack of versatility with the standard stands that are included in the box. If you want to change the orientation of your monitor, it is usually a chore rather than the quick two-second adjustment it can be with a monitor arm. Having an arm also allows you to have a deep versus a shallow display. An arm lets you push the monitor farther back than a stand would, which is crucial for those using 32-inch panels or larger on a standard-depth desk.

Another thing you have to consider is the level of your eyes' revelation. There are so many reasons why ensuring your monitor is at the right level for your eyes is fundamental, especially if you're using your computer for long periods. By subscribing, you agree to receive newsletter and marketing emails, and accept our Terms of Use and Privacy Policy. You can unsubscribe anytime.

The weight capacity is a very fundamental part, too. Most budget arms are rated for 4.4–19.8 lb (about 2–9 kg). This is perfect for almost any 27 in or 32 in 16:9 monitor, but it can be a bit of a danger zone if you have a 34 in ultra-wide or a 49 in Odyssey G9. The C-clamp is the most popular; it grips the edge of your desk. However, users who have glass desks or hollow honeycomb desks should use a reinforcement plate to prevent the arm from crushing the desk. If your desk has a cable hole or you're brave enough to drill one, then a grommet provides a much cleaner, integrated look and is physically more stable, too.

Comfort can be more important than specs

While investing in a brand-new monitor arm won't yield a high number of frames in your benchmarks, you will feel significantly better physically after a four-hour session at your desk. We spend so much time optimizing the silicone; it's about time we optimize the human sitting in front of it, too. If you're sitting at your desk for multiple hours a day, why not make it as comfortable as possible?
