item.title: Nvidia's restarting RTX 3060 production, and it's good news for gamers, great news for homelabbers
item.itunes:title: Nvidia's restarting RTX 3060 production, and it's good news for gamers, great news for homelabbers
item.itunes:author: XDA[RSS+]
item.itunes:summary:
If you told me a year ago that Nvidia would bring back a 2021 GPU in 2026, I would've assumed something had gone very wrong with the GPU market. And to be fair, that's exactly what has happened. Memory shortages, AI demand, and absurd pricing have completely reshaped what a "budget" or "mid-range" GPU even means today. So instead of pushing newer cards into a broken supply chain, Nvidia seems to be doing something unexpected: reviving the RTX 3060.

According to VideoCardz and multiple other reports, Samsung Foundry is restarting production of Nvidia's RTX 3060 GPU using its 8nm process, roughly two years after halting it. Now, this might not seem like a big deal if you already have a newer card, but it's really a workaround for a supply chain that's still under pressure from AI demand. And when you look at what the RTX 3060 offers, especially the 12GB variant, it starts to make a lot of sense.

The RTX 5060 is the nail in the coffin for 8GB graphics cards

I think we can all agree that it's time to move on.

6
By  Ty Sherback

The RTX 3060's VRAM is its biggest selling point

The RTX 5060 is more powerful, but 8GB VRAM isn't enough for gaming anymore

The RTX 3060 launched in 2021 with 12GB of VRAM, and at the time, that spec felt almost excessive for its class. Fast-forward to 2026, and that same 12GB is exactly why the card still holds up. Newer entry-level GPUs from Nvidia, like the RTX 4060 and 5060, have gone in the opposite direction, offering just 8GB of VRAM, which just isn't enough for the latest AAA titles. Higher texture settings and larger open worlds mean you're hitting VRAM limits much sooner than you'd expect. At that point, you're dealing with stutters and crashes unless you dial back your graphics settings or stick to 1080p.

With the RTX 3060, you're not getting nearly the same raw performance as the RTX 5060, but you're getting something that often matters more in real-world gaming, which is consistency. That extra VRAM gives you the headroom to run higher textures and avoid those sudden drops in smoothness that come from memory limitations. At 1080p and even 1440p with DLSS, you get a more balanced experience overall. And when a newer GPU forces you to compromise because of its 8GB limit, it starts to feel less like an upgrade and more like a trade-off.

It's a great entry-level option for home labs

12GB of VRAM gives you the headroom to actually run local LLMs

If you've tried running local LLMs on an 8GB GPU like the RTX 4060 or 5060, you already know where the problem starts. You'll hit memory limits long before you hit performance limits. Sure, many models will run, but you’re often forced to use aggressive quantization or stick to smaller models just to make them fit, which defeats the point of running them locally in the first place. That's where the RTX 3060 stands out. With 12GB of VRAM, you get enough headroom to run smaller and mid-sized models comfortably without constantly fighting memory constraints. It’s not the best option by today's standards, but for homelab use, raw performance isn't always the bottleneck.

And it's not just about LLMs either. That extra VRAM gives you the flexibility to run other workloads alongside them or experiment with different tools without constantly running into limits. Whether it's generating images with Stable Diffusion or running multiple AI services in containers at once, the RTX 3060 gives you room to actually use your setup instead of having to optimize around VRAM limits. That’s what makes it such a practical option. You're not buying it for performance, but because it lets you do more without constantly hitting a wall.

Nvidia could screw it up by relaunching the 8GB variant

But the 12GB RTX 3060 is the only variant that actually makes sense right now

Credit: Pexels

To be fair, we don't really know if Nvidia is planning to bring back the original 12GB model that launched in 2021 or the cut-down 8GB version that launched later in 2022. And that uncertainty matters more than it should. Even back then, the 8GB variant was frowned upon, and in today's market, it makes even less sense. It won't solve the VRAM limitations that the RTX 5060 already struggles with, and it would end up feeling like another compromised option rather than a meaningful alternative.

The only reason this potential relaunch is even worth talking about is because of the 12GB model. That's the version people actually care about, and more importantly, the one that still fits current use cases. If Nvidia's goal here is to ease supply constraints and offer something practical for both gamers and homelabbers, bringing back the 12GB RTX 3060 is the only move that actually makes sense. Anything else just misses the point entirely and adds another pointless GPU to a market that already has too many of them.

The RTX 3060 still makes sense because there’s no 12GB entry-level GPU

The more you look at how the GPU market has changed over the past few years, the more this situation feels backward. Of course, newer GPUs are far more powerful, but they're also more expensive, more constrained, and in some cases, less practical for the kind of workloads people actually care about today. Whether you're gaming or running local LLMs, VRAM has become one of the most important factors, and that's where current entry-level GPUs fall short. Until Nvidia or AMD offers more VRAM at this price point, the RTX 3060 will remain a worthwhile option.

Nvidia's resurrection of the RTX 3060 proves that older GPUs still matter

One of the best budget cards of the last decade gets a new lease on life

3
By  Ty Sherback
item.description:

And that uncertainty matters more than it should. Even back then, the 8GB variant was frowned upon, and in today's market, it makes even less sense. It won't solve the VRAM limitations that the RTX 5060 already struggles with, and it would end up feeling like another compromised option rather than a meaningful alternative.

The only reason this potential relaunch is even worth talking about is because of the 12GB model. That's the version people actually care about, and more importantly, the one that still fits current use cases. If Nvidia's goal here is to ease supply constraints and offer something practical for both gamers and homelabbers, bringing back the 12GB RTX 3060 is the only move that actually makes sense. Anything else just misses the point entirely and adds another pointless GPU to a market that already has too many of them.

The RTX 3060 still makes sense because there’s no 12GB entry-level GPU

The more you look at how the GPU market has changed over the past few years, the more this situation feels backward. Of course, newer GPUs are far more powerful, but they're also more expensive, more constrained, and in some cases, less practical for the kind of workloads people actually care about today. Whether you're gaming or running local LLMs, VRAM has become one of the most important factors, and that's where current entry-level GPUs fall short. Until Nvidia or AMD offers more VRAM at this price point, the RTX 3060 will remain a worthwhile option.

Nvidia's resurrection of the RTX 3060 proves that older GPUs still matter

One of the best budget cards of the last decade gets a new lease on life

3
By  Ty Sherback

item.title: LibreOffice proved that paying for Microsoft 365 was my biggest mistake
item.itunes:title: LibreOffice proved that paying for Microsoft 365 was my biggest mistake
item.itunes:author: XDA[RSS+]
item.itunes:summary:
I’ve been connected to MS Office software since my school days. Breaking away from the Microsoft 365 ecosystem always felt like a risk. Like most professionals, I was convinced that a paid subscription was the only way to stay productive and compatible. I viewed free open-source software as a compromise, something you use to save money, not to get the best results.

When I finally installed LibreOffice, I expected a clunky, frustrating experience compared to the polished world of MS Office. Instead, I discovered a powerhouse that challenged everything I thought I knew about modern document suites. It turns out that moving away from the cloud didn't slow me down; it actually made my workflow sharper.

The old UI means bad UI myth

Nostalgia that actually works

The common criticism of LibreOffice is that it looks like a time capsule from 2003. When I first fired it up, I had that same "ugh" moment; it felt like a step back to the Windows XP days. But after working for a few hours, I realized I’d fallen for the "old UI means bad UI" myth.

The interface is very responsive. Apps open quickly, menus react instantly, and common tasks like formatting text, inserting tables, or exporting files feel smooth. There’s no lag from loading web elements, no waiting for features to appear, and no unnecessary visual clutter.

What surprised me most was how predictable everything felt. The toolbars remain consistent, making it easier to build muscle memory. I didn’t have to search through multiple layers of menus just to find simple options.

Yes, it looks old compared to modern SaaS tools, but it doesn’t feel old in real use. The UI stays out of the way, and that actually helps me focus more on writing.

Compatibility is the biggest benefit

Work anywhere, with any file

One of my biggest fears about leaving the mainstream suites was file compatibility. As a freelancer, I can’t afford to send a client a document that looks broken. However, LibreOffice handled my .docx and .xlsx files with surprising ease. The formatting stayed intact, and the transition was seamless. It turns out that the "walled garden" of paid software isn't as necessary as I thought.

The real win, though, is the cross-platform freedom. I’m no longer locked into a specific ecosystem or browser. Whether I’m working on my Windows 11 setup at home or jumping to a different machine, my workflow remains the same. I can save a file locally and know it will open perfectly on Linux, macOS, or Windows without requiring a specific account login or an active internet connection. This "platform-agnostic" approach is liberating. I’m in control of my files again, and they work wherever I do. It’s not just about being compatible with Microsoft; it’s about being compatible with my own mobile lifestyle.

4 Office alternatives that let you work across devices

Part of choosing an MS Office alternative is knowing if it will work across your devices. Here are four that are free and cross-platform.

2
By  Brian Burgess

Powerful features hidden in plain sight

A power user’s toolkit in a simple box

When I started using LibreOffice, I expected basic editing features, but I quickly discovered many powerful tools that improve productivity. The advanced styles and formatting system in Writer makes a big difference. I can control headings, spacing, fonts, and layout consistently without having to manually fix everything again and again.

The template system is another time saver. I can create reusable templates for articles or documents, so I don’t have to start from scratch each time. Once set up, it keeps my workflow fast and organized.

LibreOffice also includes built-in chart tools, which make it easy to convert data into simple visual charts without needing extra software. For repetitive tasks, features like mail merge help automate document creation, especially when working with structured content.

For greater control, macro support automates repetitive actions. Even simple macros can save time when performing the same formatting steps regularly.

None of these features is flashy, but they reduce repetitive work. Over time, these small productivity improvements make LibreOffice feel much more capable than I initially expected.

It beats SaaS tools in cost and privacy

Reclaiming my wallet and my data

As I move towards using more open-source tools, I know how refreshing it feels to use one that doesn’t rely on subscriptions. With many SaaS editors, I always feel tied to monthly payments just to access my own documents. LibreOffice removes that dependency completely. I install it once, and I can use it as long as I want without worrying about renewals or feature restrictions.

Privacy is another big advantage. My documents stay on my system, not automatically synced to someone else’s server. I don’t have to think about accounts, permissions, or whether my files are being analyzed in the background. Everything works offline by default, which makes the experience feel more controlled and distraction-free.

Subscribe to our newsletter for LibreOffice tips

Want hands-on LibreOffice guidance? Subscribe to the newsletter for step-by-step tips, hidden features, ready-made templates, compatibility tricks, macro examples, and privacy-minded workflows. Practical coverage to help you use LibreOffice and open-source productivity tools.
By subscribing, you agree to receive newsletter and marketing emails, and accept our Terms of Use and Privacy Policy. You can unsubscribe anytime.

I also like that I can open my files anytime, even without internet access. There’s no loading screen or connection issue stopping me from working.

LibreOffice made me realize that sometimes simple, local tools provide more freedom than cloud-based apps.

7 features in LibreOffice that might make you switch from Microsoft Office

LibreOffice goodies that will make you rethink Microsoft Office

7
By  Parth Shah

Sometimes practical beats trendy

LibreOffice changed my perspective on what actually improves productivity. Instead of chasing modern-looking tools, I found myself appreciating software that focuses on stability, flexibility, and efficient work. The features are mature, dependable, and designed to support real workflows without constant interruptions or unnecessary complexity. Over time, I noticed fewer distractions and more consistency in how I create and manage documents. It may not try to impress visually, but it quietly supports serious work. What I expected to be a compromise turned out to be a reliable tool that helps me stay focused and productive every single day.

LibreOffice
Individual pricing
Free
Platforms
Windows, macOS, Linux

LibreOffice is one of the most complete Microsoft Office alternatives, and it's entirely free and open-source. It gives you various different tools for various use cases, including Math and Draw tools for advanced users.

Download LibreOffice
